titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Paul Yu-Kuang Sun papers
titlestmt.author: Abi Mason and Elizabeth Peters

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Monday September 16, 2019 at 22:00

did.abstract: This collection reflects the life and works of Paul Yu-Kuang Sun, a surviving prisoner of the White Terror in Taiwan from the mid-twentieth century. This collection was put together by his daughter Kang-I Sun Chang to include his writings, audio and visual copies of a selection of Sun's sermons and lectures including one at Yale University, a large selection of Sun's correspondence in both English and Chinese, and some biographical materials including published works about Paul Sun.

langmaterial.language: The materials of this collection are primarily in Chinese, with some in English. Materials with English include a few hymns, with Sun's Chinese lyrics as well, correspondence written to Sun's grandchildren, and one article in Sun's biographical materials. Two of the books in the biographical materials are written in Taiwanese.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Paul Yu-kuang Sun, previously known only as Yu-kuang Sun, was born in China but moved to Taiwan in the late 1940's at the height of China's civil war. He moved with his late wife, Yu-Chen, primarily to be closer to her family. At that time, Taiwan was known as the Republic of China. While there, Sun taught classical Chinese, civics, geography, and basic English grammar. In 1947, though, an event known as 228 changed the course of his life for a decade.

In 1947, 228 (February 28) resulted in nearly 40 years of Martial Law under the Kuomintang (KMT). 228 is the day of a protest that followed February 27 when the actions of two agents from a government agency the day prior infuriated a democracy-seeking territory. The protest was met with violence and marked the beginning of a dark era in Taiwan's history. The decade following 228, the White Terror, was filled with extreme violence even after protests stopped. During this time, tens of thousands of people disappeared and were imprisoned and executed. Paul Sun was one such prisoner, for a period of 10 years between 1950 and 1960. When Sun was imprisoned his daughter, Kang-I Sun Chang, was only 6 years old. The KMT remained in power until 1987.

When Sun was released from prison, he remained in Taiwan until the late 1970's when he moved to the United States, upon his retirement, at the urging of his children-- Kang-I Sun Chang, K.C. Sun, and Michael Sun. Sun did not return to China until 1979/1980, and was reunited with his siblings for the first time in nearly 30 years. After missing the Cultural Revolution in China, Sun was pleasantly surprised by the improved conditions of the general public and the improvements in education. As a teacher, he was especially excited by the encouragement for Chinese students to explore world affairs— a taboo in China's communist days.

In his retired years, Paul Sun devoted himself to biblical study, calligraphy, and even authored a book called "A Kernel of Wheat" in 1998. Devoted to Christianity and the Chinese Christian community, Sun spent many years writing Chinese lyrics for popular English hymns. He gave sermons and speeches about biblical literature in churches and at Yale. Following a recorded dialogue with his daughter, Kang-i wrote a book about her experience with an imprisoned father and Paul Sun's experiences during the period of White Terror. Paul Sun has been deceased since May 2007.
Note: Information on the White Terror period found from "Taiwan's White Terror: Remember the 228 Incident" by Thomas J. Shattuck (02/27/2017)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is arranged into four series: Writing, Lectures and Sermons, Correspondence, and Biographical Materials.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Paul Sun papers consist of Sun's time after imprisonment during the White Terror era in Taiwan. Except for one bible, nothing in this collection is from before 1970.
The collection is split into four series: Writings, Lectures and Sermons, Correspondence, and Biographical Materials.

The Writings include a variety of items, both academic and personal, both handwritten and printed. In this series are a few biblical charts, selections of Paul Sun's calligraphy (devotional, scriptural, and miscellaneous), including a few pieces by his sons, and copies of handwritten Classical Chinese poetry. It also includes Sun's lyrical compositions, for which Sun wrote Chinese words for English hymns. A few of his academic materials are included, beginning with his book manuscript and a print copy of "A Kernel of Wheat" (1998). One article written by Paul Sun along with two other volumes of the same journal, Peking University Journal, are in this collection (1942-1943). The last of Sun's academic pieces included are 16 essay manuscripts. Diary entries from his retirement years are present, alongside two Chinese bibles and one Concordance of Old and New Testament are the final items in the Writings.

Paul Sun's Lectures and Sermons are mostly audiocassette recordings transferred to CD format and a few DVD's, alongside a selection of Sun's handwritten sermon summaries. Audiocassette tape recordings of Gaithersberg Chinese Alliance Church bible class series on John in 1994 (1a-7b, 9 CDs). There is a DVD of Sun's bible lecture at Yale University from 2001 (this lecture is also on YouTube, and recorded on CD's; tapes 8a-9) and a DVD collection of Sun's sermons from the same year. A second set of audiocassette tape recordings are also from a bible class series Sun taught at Gaithersberg in 1995 (1a-7b, 13 CDs). Two of Paul's sermons were recorded at New Haven Chinese Alliance Church in 2001(tapes 10-11ab). The last of Paul Sun's recordings are a conversation with his daughter, Kang-I Sun Chang, about the White Terror from 2002 (tapes 12a-12b, 2 CDs).

Paul Sun's Correspondence series includes stamped envelopes, a scroll of his personally inscribed seals imprinted, and letters ranging from 1970-2004. Most of the letters are addressed to Kang-I Sun Chang with a few addressed to Sun's son K.C. and his wife Lena. Although all of the latter are written in Chinese, Sun has a set of letters written to his granddaughters Edith Chamberlin and Esther Sun in English. A group of miscellaneous letters to and from church friends is included, including a few emails discussing Paul Sun. One of these emails discusses Sun's post-humous compensation for what His imprisonment during the White Terror. Another is a student writing to Kang-I in appreciation of the Chinese hymns sang with Paul Sun's lyrics.

Lastly, Paul Sun's Biographical Materials includes the memorial service materials for Sun's Late wife Yu-Chen Chen Sun (a DVD recording of Sun's speech at the memorial is included), two copies of Cosmic Light Journal which have articles discussing Sun, and four books. These books include Kang-I's memoir Journey through the White Terror, and two Taiwanese and one Chinese version of Farewell to the White Terror" also by Kang-I.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Kang-I Sun Chang, daughter of Paul Yu-Kuang Sun, 2018.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Paul Yu-kuang Sun papers (RG 316), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library

did.unittitle: Old Testament and New Testament Book Order
Old Testament Dating, Chinese and Western Calendar
New Testament Dating, Chinese and Western Calendar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Calligraphy by Paul Yu-kuang Sun, including a piece dedicated to his son, K.C. Sun

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Calligraphy by Paul Yu-kuang Sun, with pieces dedicated to his sons K.C. Sun and Michael Kuan-chi Sun

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1 Scroll; 1 Thessalonians 5:23. Calligraphy by Paul Yu-kuang Sun.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Hymns include "Wherever He Leads, I'll Go" , "Himself" , "Where Jesus Is, 'Tis Thee" , and others.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Copies of Paul Sun's favorites are in the front, and behind are original copies of hymnal lyrics organized into the seven folders Paul Sun's compositions came in.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes: "Jesus, I Come" "Count Your Blessings" "Joy to the World"

did.unittitle:
Book Manuscript - A
Kernel of Wheat
A Kernel of Wheat by Paul Sun

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This article was written by Paul Sun. This journal was only printed 8 times due to political complications. Only the Library of Congress, Cornell University Library, Stanford University Library, and Yale have copies within the United States. "…copies of this journal have become rare items in China today. In fact I have been informed by our Chinese librarian Michael Meng that currently in the U.S. only three libraries... have holdings of this journal." -Kang-i Sun Chang

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This journal was only printed 8 times due to political complications. Only the Library of Congress, Cornell University Library, Stanford University Library, and Yale have copies within the United States. "…copies of this journal have become rare items in China today. In fact I have been informed by our Chinese librarian Michael Meng that currently in the U.S. only three libraries... have holdings of this journal." -Kang-i Sun Chang

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This journal was only printed 8 times due to political complications. Only the Library of Congress, Cornell University Library, Stanford University Library, and Yale have copies within the United States. "…copies of this journal have become rare items in China today. In fact I have been informed by our Chinese librarian Michael Meng that currently in the U.S. only three libraries... have holdings of this journal." -Kang-i Sun Chang

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Diary Entries available: Collection from 03/01/1997-11/05/1997; Copy of Article, Diary Included ; Note by Paul Sun regarding destruction of entries from 1989-2000; 08/09/2001 ; 08/23/2003; 08/24/2003; 01/23/2004; 01/25/2004
